metadata.seed: Yeah, last week I was on my way to class and my girlfriend actually called me up saying that I forgot to take out the trash. And I kind of started thinking, wow, like, I forgot to take out the trash. Something so simple, a simple chore. All I had to do was take out the trash today and I forgot to do it. And then I started to think, well, I can't do something as simple as that. Why is she still with me? Or, and why, why can't I do that? Why can't I do a simple task? Am I good enough for anyone? Am I good enough for her? Kind of starting to feel a little fearful that she would just up and leave me.

patient.query: Yeah, last week I was on my way to class and my girlfriend actually called me up saying that I forgot to take out the trash. And I kind of started thinking, wow, like, I forgot to take out the trash. Something so simple, a simple chore. All I had to do was take out the trash today and I forgot to do it. And then I started to think, well, I can't do something as simple as that. Why is she still with me? Or, and why, why can't I do that? Why can't I do a simple task? Am I good enough for anyone? Am I good enough for her? Kind of starting to feel a little fearful that she would just up and leave me.

triggers: forgot to take out the trash
automatic_thoughts: I forgot to take out the trash
I can't do something as simple as that
Why is she still with me?
Am I good enough for anyone?
Am I good enough for her?

neutral: Forgets what was going to do (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the call made you question your reliability and worth. What evidence do you have that you are capable of handling simple tasks? How might you reassure yourself that one missed chore doesn’t define your relationship?

patient.query: I guess I've always put a lot of pressure on myself to be perfect, you know? Missing that chore felt like a huge failure, like I'm just not good enough. But when I think about it, there are plenty of times I've managed things well, even if they seem small. I just wish I could stop dwelling on the mistakes and see that one little thing doesn’t mean everything’s falling apart.

automatic_thoughts: huge failure
not good enough
one little thing means everything's falling apart

neutral: Forgets what was going to do (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that one missed chore feels like a big setback, and that’s understandable. You’ve also highlighted many moments where you’ve handled things well—those successes can help balance the picture. When the thought of failure surfaces, what evidence do you have that everything is falling apart? Exploring that might help you shift focus toward the broader context.

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, when I miss something like that, I just feel this wave of anxiety, like it's all spiraling out of control. It’s like I can’t separate that one moment from everything else. I guess my mind just jumps to thinking I’m not dependable, and then I start worrying about how others see me. It feels like if I aren’t perfect, it all just falls apart.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not dependable
how others see me
if I aren't perfect, it all falls apart

llm_response.response: I hear how quickly that anxiety can feel like a wave that pulls everything else in. It’s common to worry that a single slip makes you seem unreliable or that others will judge you harshly. When perfection feels like the only safety net, the fear that “everything falls apart” can intensify. What’s one small thing you’ve tried to keep that feeling from taking over?

patient.query: I’ve tried to remind myself that it’s okay to not have everything perfect. Like, I’ll take a moment to breathe and maybe write down some things that went well during the day, even if they're small. But it’s tough because I feel like those slips overshadow any positives. I guess I still struggle to really believe those moments matter.

automatic_thoughts: slips overshadow any positives
those moments don't matter
behaviors: write down some things that went well

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure about how you’re doing, and that can make even small successes feel faint. Taking a pause to breathe and jotting down the little wins is a good step—try to let those notes remind you that progress can be incremental. If you find the routine feels scattered, maybe we can explore a simple structure that supports those moments of reflection.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, I do feel pressured, like I’m always trying to chase after this ideal version of myself. It’s kind of exhausting. Sometimes the little wins just get buried under all the thoughts about what I didn’t do, and I can’t seem to lift them out. I guess having some sort of structure could help, but part of me worries I’ll just end up messing that up too. It’s like I can’t shake off that nagging feeling that it’s never enough.

triggers: thoughts about what I didn’t do
automatic_thoughts: I can’t lift out the little wins
I’ll just end up messing that up
it’s never enough

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: Feeling like you’re constantly chasing an ideal can be draining, especially when the little wins feel hidden behind thoughts of what’s missing. It’s understandable that you’d worry about setting up a structure only to feel it might fall apart. What might a small, manageable step look like that feels doable without adding more pressure? And when you notice that nagging sense of never being enough, what thoughts come up first?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: thoughts about what I didn’t do
retrieved_concepts_used.label: triggers

patient.query: I think a small step could be just acknowledging one thing I did well each day, maybe even writing it down, but then I get worried that I’ll just start picking it apart. Like, I'll say, “Oh, that was easy; anybody could do that.” There’s this voice in my head that just keeps telling me I should be doing more or being more. It’s like I can’t help but compare myself to everyone else and feel like I’m falling short. Trying not to let

automatic_thoughts: I’ll just start picking it apart
Oh, that was easy; anybody could do that
I should be doing more or being more
I can’t help but compare myself to everyone else
I’m falling short

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a loop of doubting your own successes, which can feel exhausting. Acknowledging even small wins is still a step forward, even if the voice of doubt follows. You might try noticing when that “easy” thought pops up and gently remind yourself that recognizing a good effort is valuable in itself.

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like I want to celebrate those small wins, but then this part of me just shoots them down. It feels so constant, like I can’t catch a break from those thoughts. I guess sometimes I start to wonder if I really deserve to feel good about anything at all. It’s frustrating because I want to be proud of what I accomplish, not just feel guilty or like I need to do more.

automatic_thoughts: I don't deserve to feel good
I need to do more

llm_response.response: I hear how hard it feels when those thoughts pop up right after you try to acknowledge a win. It can be exhausting to keep pushing yourself to do more while also feeling like you’re falling short. Maybe we could explore what it would look like if you let yourself sit with that feeling of pride for a moment, without immediately adding a “more” to it.

patient.query: It’s really hard to celebrate those small wins because there's always this voice in my head that brings me down. I just feel like I can’t catch a break from those thoughts. Sometimes I wonder if I even deserve to feel good about anything at all. I don’t want to be stuck feeling guilty or like I’m always falling short. It’s just frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: I can’t catch a break from those thoughts
I don’t deserve to feel good
I’m always falling short

llm_response.response: That feeling of a relentless inner critic can make it hard to notice even small successes. It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of guilt and a sense that you’re always falling short, which can feel exhausting. Maybe we can explore ways to pause that voice and give yourself permission to acknowledge the wins you’re having.

patient.query: Yeah, that sounds good. It’s just so easy to let that inner critic take over, and I often don’t even realize when it's happening. I’d like to find a way to shift that focus for a bit, even if just to feel a little lighter. But I guess there's a part of me that worries, what if I start to feel too content? Like, what if I lose that drive to keep improving?

automatic_thoughts: inner critic takes over
what if I start to feel too content?
what if I lose that drive to keep improving?

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re already noticing when the inner critic starts to dominate, which is a helpful first step. You can try shifting focus to something lighter without losing your motivation—contentment can coexist with a drive to grow. Often that worry about “being too content” comes from a fear of making mistakes, so exploring what you fear might help you feel more balanced.

patient.query: It’s just so easy to let that inner critic take over, and I often don’t even realize when it's happening. I’d like to find a way to shift that focus for a bit, even if just to feel a little lighter. But I worry, what if I start to feel too content? Like, what if I lose that drive to keep improving?

automatic_thoughts: inner critic takes over
what if I start to feel too content?
what if I lose that drive to keep improving?

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing how the inner critic can quietly shape your day, and you’re also concerned that easing that pressure might make you feel less motivated. Maybe we can explore what a balanced sense of contentment would look like for you, and how it could coexist with your drive. What small shift might feel both lighter and still keep you moving toward your goals?
